children.title: Tutorial: fiber laser basics
children.metadata: Fiber laser architectures; CW fiber laser; pulsed fiber laser; laser beam generation; technical approaches
children.content: Fiber lasers: this tutorial provides an overview of the technical approaches most commonly used to generate laser beams. It also includes choices and various architectures that are generally used for CW or pulsed fiber laser development.

children.title: I. Fiber lasers principles:
children.metadata: Fiber laser; optical fiber amplification; Rare-Earth ions; laser diode pumping; laser cavity configuration; MOPA architecture
children.content: A fiber laser is a laser in which the amplifying media is an optical fiber. It is an active module (like an amplifier or amplifier antenna) that needs to be powered and which uses the properties of optical amplification of Rare-Earth ions.<|txt_split|>The pumping media is generally a fiber-coupled laser diode. Two kinds of architectures can be utilized:<|txt_split|>- Laser cavity configurations where the light goes in both directions through the fiber amplifying media.<|txt_split|>- MOPA configurations: (Master Oscillator Power Amplifier) where an oscillating media generates a small "seeder" signal which is amplified through the fiber amplifying media.

children.title: Figure1: Fiber laser in laser cavity configuration
children.metadata: fiber laser, laser cavity, optical components, diagram, light output
children.content: The image is a schematic diagram labeled "Figure 1: Fiber laser in laser cavity configuration," illustrating the sequential flow of electrical power through a laser system. Starting from "Electrical power," the diagram shows the path through various optical components: "Diode(s) laser," "Diode/fiber combiner," "Reflective media," "Fiber amplifying media," "Filtering/switching media," and "Reflective media," leading to the final "Output (collimator)" which emits "Light Output." Each component is connected by arrows, indicating the direction of signal or energy flow, and the entire system is enclosed within a curved line representing the laser cavity.

children.title: Figure 2: Fiber laser in a MOPA configuration (Master Oscillator Power Amplifier) - single stage version
children.metadata: Fiber laser; MOPA; single stage; optical; laser; amplifier
children.content: The diagram illustrates a single-stage Master Oscillator Power Amplifier (MOPA) fiber laser configuration. It begins with electrical power feeding into a diode laser, which then passes through an optical seeder, a diode/fiber combiner, a fiber amplifying medium, and a filtering medium, before reaching the output collimator to produce light output. Each component is clearly labeled and connected in a sequential flow.

children.title: a) Fiber amplifying media
children.metadata: fiber laser; stimulated emission; passive fibers; active fibers; rare-earth doping; Yb³⁺; Er³⁺; 915nm; 976nm; 1.5μm; 1030-1100nm; single clad fiber; double clad fiber; laser diode pumping; IX-Blue; Nufern; Coractive; NKT; Photonics Crystal Fibers
children.content: As with any laser, a fiber laser uses the principle of stimulated emission. Most fiber lasers are made from a concatenation of fiber-coupled components.<|txt_split|>The fibers associated with the various components are called "passive fibers". Passive fibers have no amplification properties. The fibers at the heart of the amplifying media are called "active fibers". Active fibers are doped with rare-earth elements (like Erbium, Ytterbium or Thulium) which perform the stimulated emission by transforming the laser diode pumping power to the laser power.<|txt_split|>The pumping wavelength required for Ytterbium \(\mathrm{(Yb3 + )}\) or Erbium \(\mathrm{(Er3 + )}\) is typically 915 or \(976\mathrm{nm}\) whereas the emission wavelength of \(\mathrm{Er3 + }\) is around \(1.5\mu \mathrm{m}\) and \(\mathrm{Yb3 + }\) between \(1030 - 1100\mathrm{nm}\).<|txt_split|>Two types of active fibers are commonly utilized:<|txt_split|>- Single clad / single mode fibers when the desired laser diode pumping power is compatible with single mode fiber coupled laser diodes (typically <1w)<|txt_split|>Double clad fibers when the laser diode pumping power is typically higher than 1W<|txt_split|>iX-Blue: French supplier - www.photonics.ixblue.com<|txt_split|>Nufern: USA(CT) supplier - www.nufern.com<|txt_split|>Coractive: Canadian supplier - www.coractive.com/<|txt_split|>- NKT: Denmark supplier - www.nktphotonicity.com [for very special PCFs - Photonics Crystal Fibers]

children.title: Figure 3: Energy levels and associated absorption/emission spectrum (only \(\mathrm{Yb}3+\) right) of an active fiber media - the combined effect of absorption and emission produces a favorable emission area for \(\mathrm{Yb}3+\) between 1030 and \(1100\mathrm{nm}\) depending on the ions population inversion ratio (the more the inversion is high, the more the emission effect and the less the absorption effect).
children.metadata: Ytterbium, absorption spectrum, emission spectrum, energy levels, active fiber, inversion ratio, 1030–1100nm
children.content: The image presents a graph showing the energy levels and associated absorption (red curve) and emission (blue curve) spectra of Ytterbium (Yb3+) in an active fiber medium. The x-axis represents wavelength in nanometers (nm), ranging from 850 to 1130 nm, while the y-axis indicates the transition cross-section in units of 10^10 cm^2. The absorption spectrum peaks around 980 nm, and the emission spectrum peaks near 1030 nm, with the emission intensity decreasing toward 1100 nm. The accompanying text explains that the combined absorption and emission create a favorable emission area for Yb3+ between 1030 and 1100 nm, where the population inversion ratio is highest—indicating stronger emission. The higher the inversion ratio, the more the emission effect.

children.title: b) Pump laser diodes
children.metadata: fiber coupled pump laser diodes; AlGaAs and graphene oxide; 800-1000nm emission; 915/976nm absorption; single mode vs multimode; butterfly package; TEC cooler; fBG and VBG stabilization; $1500-$2000 pricing; AeroDIODE drivers; R&D to production integration
children.content: Pump laser diodes that are used for fiber lasers are fiber coupled device generally based on AlGaAs (AlGaAs) and graphene oxide. The technology emitting in the 800-1000nm range (most often 915 or 976nm = absorption spectrum Figure 2).<|txt_split|>They can be separated into two major families:<|txt_split|>1-Single mode fiber coupled laser diodes where the light coming from a small edge-emitting laser diode is focused into a "6 \(\mu\)m fiber laser core. This type of laser diode is generally assembled in a butterfly package with a TEC cooler integrated into the package (the trend today being towards 1000-1200 GHz) and a single fiber core. The fiber core has been made from 300 nm and a 1.5 W of output power. They are used to pump single clad active fibers (see Figure 4).<|txt_split|>The major suppliers for 915/976nm single mode pump laser diodes are companies which developed their businesses at the end of the nineties for fiber amplifiers used in the Telecom market (EDFAs: Erbium Doped Fiber Amplifiers). They offer both a high level of reliability and a moderate cost due to their high production volumes.<|txt_split|>2-Multimode fiber coupled laser diodes used in fiber lasers are generally based on broad area side emitting laser diode chips. These can also be separated in two categories:<|txt_split|>- Single emitters laser diodes where a single laser diode chip of typically \(15\mathrm{W}\) is coupled into generally a 105 (core)/125\(\mu\)m(clad) multimode fiber<|txt_split|>- Multi emitters laser diodes which are based on multiple laser diode chips which are coupled into a similar fiber and offer power levels scalable up to several hundred watts.<|txt_split|>Note that, as observed in Figure 3, the absorption spectrum of a rare-earth ion like \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}\) at \(976\mathrm{nm}\) is narrow and requires a stabilized laser diode absorption spectrum. This wavelength stability requires the laser diode temperature to be controlled and often the laser diode to include an additional wavelength stabilizing element. This element is generally a fBG (Fiber Bragg Grating) for single mode laser diodes. The fBG is usually situated roughly 1 meter from the laser diode), or a VBG (Volume Bragg Grating) for multimode laser diodes. The VBG is essentially a specialized piece of glass integrated into the laser diode package.<|txt_split|>The price of these diodes is typically in the range of $1500 for single mode laser diodes, $500 for multimode single emitters and $2000 for multi-emitter laser diodes.<|txt_split|>Driving a laser diode and taking into account all of the constraints and requirements specific to a fiber laser is a difficult task which requires specialized products. Here are two laser diode drivers which have been specially designed for fiber laser diode driving and are compatible with both R&D and full fiber laser product integration:<|txt_split|>- The Central board from AeroDIODE (fiber laser diode driver) which acts as a control center for nearly all types of fiber laser architectures. This driver board includes 2 single mode laser diode drivers and TEC controllers working in both CW and pulsed regime and 6 photodiode measurement circuits for fiber laser power monitoring.<|txt_split|>- The CCM (Cool and Control Multimode) from AeroDIODE (high power laser diode driver) is fully optimized for driving one or several multimode pump laser diodes (either single element or multiple element devices) including the high power TEC controller and air cooling setup.

children.title: c) Optical seeder
children.metadata: Fiber laser seeder architectures; MOPA laser systems; laser diode seeders; Bragg grating stabilization; wavelength tuning; Dopant gain medium; pulse regime amplification; DFB/DBR laser diodes; emission bandwidth; semiconductor cavity; butterfly package; high gain amplification
children.content: Fiber lasers with MOPA architectures have a seeder section which determines the initial optical properties to be amplified through the various amplifying stages (Figure 2).<|txt_split|>The optical seeder section is where the major differences in fiber laser architectures occur. There are many seeder architectures. These include a laser diode driven in CW or pulsed mode, a laser diode feeding an external high speed modulation device (see our tutorials: 'high speed fiber modulator basics and fiber-coupled laser diode basics'), a special Q-switch cavity, a mode locked cavity, a crystal structure, and a microchip and many other approaches. These various seeder-dependent architectures are described further in \(\S\) III (page 11). Only the direct laser diode part is described in this paragraph.<|txt_split|>As described in Figure 3, only the wavelength compatible with the amplifier gain medium is relevant for the Dopant. The Dopant is a function of the pulse width and thus wavelength ranges that are amplified by the dopants typically embedded in the active fiber media.<|txt_split|>A summary of several common types of laser diode seeders follows:<|txt_split|>- A "standard" laser diode seeder is a common partially reflecting semiconductor cavity which is integrated into a 14-pin butterfly package. The wavelength emission spectrum is highly dependent on an additional Bragg grating. The emission bandwidth is typically \(3\mathrm{-5nm}\) without any Bragg grating, whereas it is much narrower (\(\sim0.1\mathrm{nm}\)) with a Bragg grating. The wavelength spectrum temperature tuning coefficient is typically \(0.35\mathrm{nm} / \mathrm{C}\) without any Bragg whereas it is much less with a Bragg grating.<|txt_split|>- DFB or DBR laser diode seeder devices have the Bragg grating wavelength stabilization (GBS) technique. This technique is used to stabilize the Bragg gratings in a device. This provides a narrower emission wavelength of typically \(2\mathrm{MHz}\) for a DBF (i.e., \(\sim 10^{-3}\mathrm{nm}\)).<|txt_split|>When used in pulse regime, these laser diodes can be amplified up to very high gain levels.

children.title: d) Diode / fiber combiner
children.metadata: Fiber laser coupling; Single mode vs multimode; TAP-couplers; Thin film WDM; PCF fibers; Bragg grating; ASE filtering; Laser cavity mirror; Asian suppliers; High power components
children.content: Coupling the light from a fiber coupled laser diode into an active fiber is a complicated process. This is a complex and difficult configuration (Figure 2) where both the input seeder source and the pump light need to be injected.<|txt_split|>The components used in this coupling process are different for a single mode fiber coupled laser diode than they are for a multimode fiber coupled laser diode.<|txt_split|>For single mode laser diode pumping, two types of components are generally used:<|txt_split|>- TAP-couplers/WDM's: These are based on the principle of fusing and turning two fibers together so that the modes of both fibers can be coupled up to the moment the targeted performances are reached. One big limitation here comes from the minimum wavelength separation between two fibers, which is not possible from two different wavelengths. Several hundred nanometers difference is generally necessary.<|txt_split|>- Thin film WDM's (Wavelength Division Multiplexers): These are the solution to employ when Tap-couplers do not work. These components are based on the technology of thin films dichroic Transmission/reflection performance. The light is not actually in the fiber when inside the component, but we consider it a fibered component.<|txt_split|>Combining the light from one or several multimode fiber-coupled laser diodes with a single mode seeder is a very difficult task. This becomes even more difficult when using special fibers like PCFs (Photonics Crystal Fibers). Many technologies use the principle of fusing several fibers together in a special glass tube.<|txt_split|>Many Asian suppliers offer such components. Four highly reputable suppliers are listed below. The following table shows the number of companies listed in single mode components whereas the two last are more known for their multimode composites.<|txt_split|>DK Photonics (China):www.dkphotonics.com<|txt_split|>ITF technologies (Canada): www.itfttechnologies.com<|txt_split|>Lightcom (China): www.lightcomm.com<|txt_split|>ALPhANOV (France) offers also some specialty components associated with complex high power PCF fibers: www.alphanov.com<|txt_split|>e) Reflective media / Filtering media<|txt_split|>Reflective media - Bragg grating mirror<|txt_split|>A key component which is widely used in fiber lasers is a Bragg grating, which is periodic or aperiodic and has been extensively studied for the construction of an optical fiber. These are generally made by illuminating a germanosilicate fiber with UV light.<|txt_split|>The Bragg grating allows any type of reflection/transmission spectrum depending on the way the fiber UV illumination is manufactured.<|txt_split|>For laser cavities as described in Figure1, a Bragg grating is used to provide a total or partial reflector mirror to build a laser cavity.<|txt_split|>Filtering media - Bragg gratings filters<|txt_split|>Fiber lasers (especially MOPA configurations) face an unwanted effect called ASE (Amplified Spontaneous Emission). This is a bidirectional amplification effect starting from a low level of light and increasing the intensity of the emission. The amplification of the seerer light. An ASE has a spectrum correlated with the gain spectrum of the rare-earth dopant. It is thus very broad, and the intensity increases non-linearly along the fiber length.<|txt_split|>Since the seeder spectrum is generally much narrower than the ASE spectrum, it is relevant to add a higher gain at the same wavelength. This is because the ASE spectrum has a lower gain than the ASE spectrum and is higher than the gain at these wavelengths, but still allowing the seeder light to go through.

The red line represents the ASE gain spectrum before and after band-pass filtering, while the blue line shows the filtered ASE gain spectrum. The graph highlights how the filtering process reduces the ASE gain, especially around the 1064nm region, demonstrating the effect of the Bragg grating on the spectral profile. The reference citation is provided as (Nunamaker, Kenji et al., J. Phys. Conf. Ser., 228 (2010)).

subnode.title: d) Diode / fiber combiner_1
subnode.metadata: Fiber laser coupling; Single mode vs multimode; TAP-couplers; WDM components; Wavelength separation limitation
subnode.content: Coupling the light from a fiber coupled laser diode into an active fiber is a complicated process. This is a complex and difficult configuration (Figure 2) where both the input seeder source and the pump light need to be injected.The components used in this coupling process are different for a single mode fiber coupled laser diode than they are for a multimode fiber coupled laser diode.For single mode laser diode pumping, two types of components are generally used:- TAP-couplers/WDM's: These are based on the principle of fusing and turning two fibers together so that the modes of both fibers can be coupled up to the moment the targeted performances are reached. One big limitation here comes from the minimum wavelength separation between two fibers, which is not possible from two different wavelengths. Several hundred nanometers difference is generally necessary.

subnode.title: d) Diode / fiber combiner_2
subnode.metadata: Thin film WDM; Tap-couplers; dichroic transmission; fibered component; multimode laser diodes; single mode seeder; PCFs; fiber fusion; glass tube
subnode.content: - Thin film WDM's (Wavelength Division Multiplexers): These are the solution to employ when Tap-couplers do not work. These components are based on the technology of thin films dichroic Transmission/reflection performance. The light is not actually in the fiber when inside the component, but we consider it a fibered component.Combining the light from one or several multimode fiber-coupled laser diodes with a single mode seeder is a very difficult task. This becomes even more difficult when using special fibers like PCFs (Photonics Crystal Fibers). Many technologies use the principle of fusing several fibers together in a special glass tube.

subnode.title: d) Diode / fiber combiner_4
subnode.metadata: Bragg grating; fiber laser component; UV illumination; optical fiber; reflection/transmission spectrum; laser cavity mirror
subnode.content: Reflective media - Bragg grating mirrorA key component which is widely used in fiber lasers is a Bragg grating, which is periodic or aperiodic and has been extensively studied for the construction of an optical fiber. These are generally made by illuminating a germanosilicate fiber with UV light.The Bragg grating allows any type of reflection/transmission spectrum depending on the way the fiber UV illumination is manufactured.For laser cavities as described in Figure1, a Bragg grating is used to provide a total or partial reflector mirror to build a laser cavity.

subnode.title: d) Diode / fiber combiner_5
subnode.metadata: ASE; Bragg gratings; Fiber lasers; MOPA configuration; Gain spectrum; Seeder light; Non-linear amplification
subnode.content: Filtering media - Bragg gratings filtersFiber lasers (especially MOPA configurations) face an unwanted effect called ASE (Amplified Spontaneous Emission). This is a bidirectional amplification effect starting from a low level of light and increasing the intensity of the emission. The amplification of the seerer light. An ASE has a spectrum correlated with the gain spectrum of the rare-earth dopant. It is thus very broad, and the intensity increases non-linearly along the fiber length.Since the seeder spectrum is generally much narrower than the ASE spectrum, it is relevant to add a higher gain at the same wavelength. This is because the ASE spectrum has a lower gain than the ASE spectrum and is higher than the gain at these wavelengths, but still allowing the seeder light to go through.

children.title: f) Switching media
children.metadata: Cavity laser; MOPA laser; Time-related collisional loss; AOM modulation; EOM modulation; Hybrid modulation; Mode-lock architecture
children.content: Historically, the two alternative configurations of Figure 1 (Cavity) and Figure 2 (MOPA) were respectively associated with CW fiber lasers and pulsed fiber lasers. This is not the case anymore as many high-power CW architectures use the principle of MOPA amplification and some pulsed configurations are made in a unique cavity with no amplifier afterward.<|txt_split|>Making a pulsed fiber laser in a cavity configuration requires a time related collisional loss media which is usually the result of a combination of the optical and mechanical properties (mode-lock architecture principles described in II) or a switching media for active loss synchronization.<|txt_split|>Switching media can either be an AOM (Acousto-Optic Modulator), an EOM (electro-optic modulator), or a hybrid (hybrid) optical modulator. The hybrid modulation technologies are compared in another Tutorial called "High Speed fiber modulator basics".

children.content: Fiber lasers generally also include some monitoring components to permanently verify its level of power at every stage. Fibered photodiodes are such components. It is often necessary to understand how a photodiode behaves with time (speed) and other parameters such as temperature etc. Having a well detailed reference datasheet is useful, as many photodiode technical parameters are similar from one to another. A well detailed reference InGaAs photodiode 10 pages datasheet is the EPM 6xx Series from Lumentum.<|txt_split|>Typically, a pulsed MOPA high power fiber laser requires at least 5 photodiodes which are involved in the fiber laser monitoring and various securities and internal interlock:<|txt_split|>- 1 photodiode to control the average power of the seeder<|txt_split|>- 1 fast photodiode to "watch-dog" the seeder pulses and start some fast securities when a pulse is missing.<|txt_split|>- 1 photodiode at every stage which control the average power, and especially on both sides of a pulse-picking unit if it is part of the<|txt_split|>- 1 "BFM" (Back-Facet Monitor) to control the power which is going back to the fiber laser<|txt_split|>- 1 or 2 output photodiodes (fibered or not fibered when the level of power does not allow fibered devices).<|txt_split|>All these photodiodes are involved in some special starting and switch off procedure to avoid the fiber laser to get damaged.<|txt_split|>The major destructive effect which needs to be well controlled within a single or multiple stage MOPA configuration comes from potential situation where the pumping laser diode are still ON while the seeder has no emission. This immediately creates giant pulses which induces a permanent damage within the fiber laser.<|txt_split|>The central board of AeroDiODE has "50 special electronics functionalities correlated with all the components described above to make nearly any type of the fiber laser architecture described below.

children.title: Fiber laser diode driver pin assignment and functionalities overview :
a) Introduction
children.metadata: fiber laser architectures; constraint overview; simulation software; isolators omitted; monitoring photodiodes omitted
children.content: The various architectures shown below are intended to provide the reader with an overview of the typical constraints which dictate some typical fiber laser architectures. Of course, many variants do exist and good simulation software like RP-Fiber-Power is mandatory to determine the best architecture.<|txt_split|>Note that for clarity purposes, the isolators (at every stage) and monitoring photodiodes are not show in the figures below.

children.title: b) CW fiber lasers
children.metadata: CW fiber laser; end-pumped cavity; Bragg grating; optical output characteristics; long fiber length; Yb3+ emission; 1030-1100 nm; optical bandgap; seeder laser diode; multi-stage amplification; mode stripper; ASE filter
children.content: CW fiber lasers are generally made by an end-pumped simple Bragg grating based cavity with some optical parameters. Here there are specific requirements concerning the optical output characteristics (i.e. wavelength etc.).<|txt_split|>A typical CW fiber laser architecture is shown in Figure 14 below. A multiple pumped cavity is used to generate a high-power level. It is generally preferable to take a very long length of fiber to maximize absorption and avoid any remnant light on both sides of the grating which may be coming from the pumps on the other side.<|txt_split|>Choosing a long length of fiber reduces the overall inversion level within the active fiber. As explained in Figure 3, the combined effect of absorption and emission makes the most favorable emission area for Yb3+ between 1030 and \(1100\mathrm{nm}\) depending on the population inversion ratio of the active fiber. The optical bandgap is also affected by the wavelength of a CW class CW fiber laser is generally higher than 1080 or even \(1100\mathrm{nm}\).<|txt_split|>A typical high-power fiber laser is shown in Figure 15. A seeder laser diode source is used, and multiple amplification stages are necessary to reach a high-power level. Note that a mode stripper is often necessary to remove the remnant pump power after the active fiber. One or several ASE filters are also often necessary to remove Amplified Emission and keep a good signal over noise ratio.

subnode.content: Choosing a long length of fiber reduces the overall inversion level within the active fiber. As explained in Figure 3, the combined effect of absorption and emission makes the most favorable emission area for Yb3+ between 1030 and \(1100\mathrm{nm}\) depending on the population inversion ratio of the active fiber. The optical bandgap is also affected by the wavelength of a CW class CW fiber laser is generally higher than 1080 or even \(1100\mathrm{nm}\).A typical high-power fiber laser is shown in Figure 15. children.title: c) Pulsed fiber lasers (millisecond/microsecond range)
children.metadata: Population inversion time; Pump power modulation; Fiber laser architecture; Thermal properties; Duty cycle design
children.content: The time scale of population inversion of \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}\) ions when applying high pumping power is typically in the range of 20-200 msec (typically “\(\sim 10\%\)\(^{3+}\) for the \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}\) lifetime). This means that when one wants to get some pulses which are longer than this, pump power can be electronically pulsed and the same behavior as for a CW laser is achieved during these pulses. This means that the typical architectures of the two systems are different. The main difference between fiber lasers and CW fiber lasers are the same as for CW fiber lasers. Only the overall thermal properties are different, which may allow choosing different components when designing low duty cycle fiber lasers.

children.title: d) Pulsed fiber laser (nanosecond range)
children.metadata: nanosecond fiber lasers; optical pumping; toilet flush analogy; CW pumping; ASE power; ion density; MOPA architecture; single cavity Q-switch; pulse generation; pump pulsing
children.content: When one wants to generate short pulses in the 1 ns-10 \(\mu\)s range, we enter in the world of nanosecond pulsed fiber lasers.<|txt_split|>As described above, the properties of \(\Upsilon\beta^{+}\) ions are not sufficient to generate such pulses by means of a pump. In fact, it is necessary to keep a continuous pump power and find a way to generate the pulses differently.<|txt_split|>The comparison with the flush of a toilet is relevant here. Optical pumping is the water coming in the tube, which is used to drive the water through the tube. The optical pump has a special optical pulse at desired frequency. This comparison allows us to understand a few important elements:<|txt_split|>- When considering CW pumping (continuous water flow), one can imagine that a minimum flush frequency is required otherwise the water coming in continuously will overflow. This minimum frequency for a fiber laser is typically \(5\mathrm{kHz}\). Below this frequency, some ASE power starts to come out of the fiber between pulses.<|txt_split|>- We can also note that an important parameter is the number of ions in the active fiber. This number is directly correlated with the absolute maximum energy that can be obtained at every pulse (toilet tank size determines the absolute maximum water that can be obtained, while to the toilet bowl it is less water because a separated mechanism makes the water stop filling the toilet bowl).<|txt_split|>- The latest generation fiber laser electronic circuits are used to apply pump pulsing in MOPA (Amplified) nanosecond fiber laser architectures (see a description of such electronics in §1V p 20). Again, the comparison with a toilet flush is relevant when considering the two effects of a tank filling which start and stop separated from the action to flush to fill the toilet bowl.<|txt_split|>There are two very different types of architectures, as it has already been described in the beginning of this document (Figure1 and Figure 2): single fiber laser cavity and MOPA (Master Oscillator Power Amplifier). Most of the nanosecond fiber laser architectures are based on MOPA architectures, except for the single cavity Q-switch architecture.

children.title: Q-switch nanosecond Fiber lasers
children.metadata: Q-switched fiber lasers; AOM; optoelectronic modulator; pulse generation; simple architecture; low modularity; optical parameter control
children.content: Q-switch fiber lasers are obtained when a specialized fast switching/modulating component is integrated in the fiber laser cavity. This component can be either an AOM (Acousto-Optic Modulator) or an optoelectronic modulator (Optoelectronic modulator). See our Tutorial "Fiber optic modulator basics" for a detailed description of such components. Switching the component to low loss level releases a high energy pulse of typically a few nanoseconds.<|txt_split|>An example of a Q-switch architecture is shown in Figure 18. A fast AOM is used to apply the loss.<|txt_split|>The pro of such an architecture is that it is quite simple as it requires few components. However, the modularity and ability to control optical parameters is rather low.

The combined light travels to an active fiber, which is central to the system. The active fiber connects to an Acousto-Optic Modulator (AOM) driven by an AOM RF driver, which is then connected to a Bragg mirror. A separate Bragg mirror is shown connected to the AOM, indicating optical feedback or control. The entire setup demonstrates how laser diodes, combiners, active fiber, AOM, and Bragg mirrors are integrated for pulse generation in a Q-switched fiber laser.

children.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers
children.metadata: fiber laser architecture; ASE filtering; gain switching; spectral locking; pulse shape deformation; nonlinear effects; laser diode drivers; AWG modulation; EOM phase modulation; high-power pumping
children.content: Most of the engraving lasers sold today are based on this architecture. A seed laser diode at, for example, 1064nm is pulsed by some specialized fast current pulsing electronic circuit before being amplified with several stages of active fibers. A typical amplification stage generates between 10 and 20 dB of gain. Above that level of gain, some unwanted ASE (Amplified Spontaneous Emission) effects amplify undesired wavelengths. It is therefore desired to have a multiple stage amplifier with ASE filters between each stage instead of maximizing the amplification gain of a given stage.<|txt_split|>Four important effects need to be considered when looking at fiber laser amplification of pulsed laser diodes:<|txt_split|>1. Laser diode gain switching effect: when applying current to a laser diode, at the initial part of the pulse (picosecond time), some amount of energy is stored in the gain medium. This energy is subsequently realized in the form of a short pulse. This pulse is typically on the order of a 100 ps pulse duration. This short pulse can be viewed either as an opportunity, such as when trying to produce very short (~100 ps) pulses, or as a problem when considering nanosecond range pulses being amplified up to high energy levels.<|txt_split|>2. Evolution of the emission spectrum of pulsed laser diodes: When directly pulsing a laser diode, the user should consider two undesirable spectral effects:<|txt_split|>- The first is correlated to the time required for the laser diode to "lock" onto its Bragg locking element. This locking is immediate for a DFB but often requires more than 100 msec for a Bragg grating based laser diode. In other words, when pulsing Bragg grating stabilized laser diodes, the first nanosecond produces a broad emission spectrum as if there was no Bragg grating. Some suppliers offer an intermediate solution called "Bragg close to the chip" which take only a few nanoseconds to lock.<|txt_split|>- Another unavoidable effect comes from coupling of the frequency/phase spectrum and intensity profile. More specifically, the emission spectrum changes over the pulse length and this can sometimes be a problem. External modulation with, for example, an SOA offers a smart solution to avoid this effect. See our Tutorial: fiber intensity modulator basics for a detailed comparison of the four commonly used technologies for modulating laser light externally.<|txt_split|>3. Deformation of the pulse shape: when considering MOFA fiber laser architectures with high pulse power, it is important to understand how these structures depend on the dopant population inversion levels. These levels reduce over the pulse duration:<|txt_split|>Therefore, a deformation of the pulse occurs which prevents a nice square pulse shape at the output. Some pulsed laser diode drivers can adjust the shape of a given pulse to pre-compensate for this effect and reach the desired pulse shape at the output of the last amplification stage.<|txt_split|>4. Fiber nonlinear effects: Fiber amplifiers concentrate the light in a small diameter core and allow us to increase the power density up to very high levels. This can become a major problem when considering high pulse peak powers as many optical non-linear effects appear above a certain level of peak power and spectral densities. These effects, like SBS (Stimulated Brillouin Scattering) or SRS (Stimulated Raman Scattering), tend to broaden both the emission spectrum and the pulse duration. SBS is an effect which depends non-linearly on the spectral density. Choosing a broader emission seeder and avoiding narrow spectral width DFBs can be a good choice to reach higher peak powers when utilizing nanosecond pulses. Another solution is to use an EOM (Electro-Optic) phase Modulator. This broadens the emission spectrum while keeping the nice spectral stability of a DFB.<|txt_split|>When discussing laser diode driver electronics, it may be helpful to the reader to be aware of the three products shown below:<|txt_split|>The central board from AeroDiODE has one laser diode channel optimized for low noise CW driving and one channel optimized for both CW and nanosecond short pulsing. It also contains many fiber laser relevant functions, such as multiple photodiode inputs. The central board acts as a "control center" for a fiber laser. Central boards are also able to handle pulse pumping functions which are very helpful when designing low repetition rate, high energy systems. Refer to this product page: fiber laser diode driver.<|txt_split|>The Shaper board is another driver offered by AeroDiODE that can solve two of the four issues detailed in the sections above: it can pre-compensate the pulse shape and has a special Gain-Switch function. The first part of the board is used to adjust device parameters, which is its internal AWG (Arbitrary Waveform Generator) generator. One point every 500 ps with 48 dB waveguide also contains 3 pulse delay generator outputs. See this product page: high speed laser diode driver.<|txt_split|>The CCM Module is the third laser diode driver offered by AeroDiODE. This high-power driver is designed to control the multimode single and multi-emitter laser diodes which are referenced in all the fiber laser architectures described above. It is fully dedicated for driving one or several multimode pump laser diodes (either single elements or multiple elements) including the high-power TEC controller with an air-cooling setup. It contains many functions to drive any of this class of laser in an optimized and compact air-cooling setup. See the product page: high power laser diode driver.

Each stage includes components such as the MOPA, amplifier, and fiber combiner, with the seed pulse progressively being amplified across the stages to achieve higher power output.

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_1
subnode.metadata: engraving lasers; laser diode; current pulsing; fiber amplification; ASE filtering; gain optimization
subnode.content: Most of the engraving lasers sold today are based on this architecture. A seed laser diode at, for example, 1064nm is pulsed by some specialized fast current pulsing electronic circuit before being amplified with several stages of active fibers. A typical amplification stage generates between 10 and 20 dB of gain. Above that level of gain, some unwanted ASE (Amplified Spontaneous Emission) effects amplify undesired wavelengths. It is therefore desired to have a multiple stage amplifier with ASE filters between each stage instead of maximizing the amplification gain of a given stage.

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_2
subnode.metadata: Laser diode gain switching; Picosecond pulse storage; Short pulse generation; Pulse duration optimization; Amplification challenges
subnode.content: Four important effects need to be considered when looking at fiber laser amplification of pulsed laser diodes:1. Laser diode gain switching effect: when applying current to a laser diode, at the initial part of the pulse (picosecond time), some amount of energy is stored in the gain medium. This energy is subsequently realized in the form of a short pulse. This pulse is typically on the order of a 100 ps pulse duration. This short pulse can be viewed either as an opportunity, such as when trying to produce very short (~100 ps) pulses, or as a problem when considering nanosecond range pulses being amplified up to high energy levels.

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_3
subnode.metadata: Bragg locking delay; Spectral broadening in pulsed laser diodes; DFB vs. Bragg grating stabilization; Bragg close to chip solution
subnode.content: 2. Evolution of the emission spectrum of pulsed laser diodes: When directly pulsing a laser diode, the user should consider two undesirable spectral effects:- The first is correlated to the time required for the laser diode to "lock" onto its Bragg locking element. This locking is immediate for a DFB but often requires more than 100 msec for a Bragg grating based laser diode. In other words, when pulsing Bragg grating stabilized laser diodes, the first nanosecond produces a broad emission spectrum as if there was no Bragg grating. Some suppliers offer an intermediate solution called "Bragg close to the chip" which take only a few nanoseconds to lock.

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_4
subnode.metadata: pulse shape deformation; frequency-phase coupling; intensity profile variation; external modulation; SOA modulation; fiber laser architecture; dopant inversion dynamics
subnode.content: - Another unavoidable effect comes from coupling of the frequency/phase spectrum and intensity profile. More specifically, the emission spectrum changes over the pulse length and this can sometimes be a problem. External modulation with, for example, an SOA offers a smart solution to avoid this effect. See our Tutorial: fiber intensity modulator basics for a detailed comparison of the four commonly used technologies for modulating laser light externally.3. Deformation of the pulse shape: when considering MOFA fiber laser architectures with high pulse power, it is important to understand how these structures depend on the dopant population inversion levels. These levels reduce over the pulse duration:

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_5
subnode.metadata: pulse deformation; pre-compensation; fiber nonlinear effects; SBS; SRS; spectral broadening; EOM phase modulator; DFB laser; peak power management
subnode.content: Therefore, a deformation of the pulse occurs which prevents a nice square pulse shape at the output. Some pulsed laser diode drivers can adjust the shape of a given pulse to pre-compensate for this effect and reach the desired pulse shape at the output of the last amplification stage.4. Fiber nonlinear effects: Fiber amplifiers concentrate the light in a small diameter core and allow us to increase the power density up to very high levels. This can become a major problem when considering high pulse peak powers as many optical non-linear effects appear above a certain level of peak power and spectral densities. These effects, like SBS (Stimulated Brillouin Scattering) or SRS (Stimulated Raman Scattering), tend to broaden both the emission spectrum and the pulse duration. SBS is an effect which depends non-linearly on the spectral density. Choosing a broader emission seeder and avoiding narrow spectral width DFBs can be a good choice to reach higher peak powers when utilizing nanosecond pulses. Another solution is to use an EOM (Electro-Optic) phase Modulator. This broadens the emission spectrum while keeping the nice spectral stability of a DFB.

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_6
subnode.metadata: laser diode driver; central board; low noise CW driving; nanosecond pulsing; fiber laser functions; pulse pumping; control center; high energy systems
subnode.content: When discussing laser diode driver electronics, it may be helpful to the reader to be aware of the three products shown below:The central board from AeroDiODE has one laser diode channel optimized for low noise CW driving and one channel optimized for both CW and nanosecond short pulsing. It also contains many fiber laser relevant functions, such as multiple photodiode inputs. The central board acts as a "control center" for a fiber laser. Central boards are also able to handle pulse pumping functions which are very helpful when designing low repetition rate, high energy systems. Refer to this product page: fiber laser diode driver.

subnode.title: MOPA nanosecond fiber lasers_7
subnode.metadata: Shaper board; pre-compensation; Gain-Switch; AWG generator; pulse delay; CCM Module; multimode laser diode; high-power driver; TEC controller; air-cooling setup
subnode.content: The Shaper board is another driver offered by AeroDiODE that can solve two of the four issues detailed in the sections above: it can pre-compensate the pulse shape and has a special Gain-Switch function. The first part of the board is used to adjust device parameters, which is its internal AWG (Arbitrary Waveform Generator) generator. One point every 500 ps with 48 dB waveguide also contains 3 pulse delay generator outputs. See this product page: high speed laser diode driver.The CCM Module is the third laser diode driver offered by AeroDiODE. This high-power driver is designed to control the multimode single and multi-emitter laser diodes which are referenced in all the fiber laser architectures described above. It is fully dedicated for driving one or several multimode pump laser diodes (either single elements or multiple elements) including the high-power TEC controller with an air-cooling setup. It contains many functions to drive any of this class of laser in an optimized and compact air-cooling setup. See the product page: high power laser diode driver.

subnode.title: d) Pulsed fiber laser (nanosecond range)_1
subnode.metadata: nanosecond pulsed fiber lasers; optical pumping; pump power; pulse generation; toilet flush analogy
subnode.content: When one wants to generate short pulses in the 1 ns-10 \(\mu\)s range, we enter in the world of nanosecond pulsed fiber lasers.As described above, the properties of \(\Upsilon\beta^{+}\) ions are not sufficient to generate such pulses by means of a pump. In fact, it is necessary to keep a continuous pump power and find a way to generate the pulses differently.The comparison with the flush of a toilet is relevant here. Optical pumping is the water coming in the tube, which is used to drive the water through the tube. The optical pump has a special optical pulse at desired frequency. This comparison allows us to understand a few important elements:

subnode.title: d) Pulsed fiber laser (nanosecond range)_2
subnode.metadata: CW pumping; flush frequency; ASE power; ion count; maximum energy
subnode.content: - When considering CW pumping (continuous water flow), one can imagine that a minimum flush frequency is required otherwise the water coming in continuously will overflow. This minimum frequency for a fiber laser is typically \(5\mathrm{kHz}\). Below this frequency, some ASE power starts to come out of the fiber between pulses.- We can also note that an important parameter is the number of ions in the active fiber. This number is directly correlated with the absolute maximum energy that can be obtained at every pulse (toilet tank size determines the absolute maximum water that can be obtained, while to the toilet bowl it is less water because a separated mechanism makes the water stop filling the toilet bowl).

subnode.title: d) Pulsed fiber laser (nanosecond range)_3
subnode.metadata: MOPA laser architecture; fiber laser electronics; pump pulsing; single cavity Q-switch; toilet flush analogy
subnode.content: - The latest generation fiber laser electronic circuits are used to apply pump pulsing in MOPA (Amplified) nanosecond fiber laser architectures (see a description of such electronics in §1V p 20). Again, the comparison with a toilet flush is relevant when considering the two effects of a tank filling which start and stop separated from the action to flush to fill the toilet bowl.There are two very different types of architectures, as it has already been described in the beginning of this document (Figure1 and Figure 2): single fiber laser cavity and MOPA (Master Oscillator Power Amplifier). Most of the nanosecond fiber laser architectures are based on MOPA architectures, except for the single cavity Q-switch architecture.

children.title: e) Pulsed fiber lasers (picosecond range)
children.metadata: Picosecond fiber lasers; pulse width; MOPA architecture; seeder design; nanosecond laser; pulse generation
children.content: Picosecond fiber lasers operate in the 10 picoseconds to a nanosecond pulse width range. These are the most common laser that is generally associated with special architectures that generate pulse widths below 10 picoseconds.<|txt_split|>These picosecond lasers are typically very similar to the MOPA nanosecond fiber laser architectures described in Figure 17. The only major difference comes from the seeder, as it becomes very difficult to get a very short pulse by directly pulsing the laser diode. Therefore, we can separate picosecond fiber laser architectures into 3 categories:

children.title: Gain switch direct diode seeder:
children.metadata: picosecond fiber laser; gain switch effect; laser diode; amplification stages; pulse stability; fiber coupling; electronic driver; ASE filtering
children.content: The simplest configuration to get a picosecond fiber laser is to use the gain switch effect of a laser diode (see page 14). This effect occurs during the first 100 picoseconds of the optical pulse when a short electronic pulse is applied to the laser diode (see Figure 18). Companies like Picocquant or NKT (ex OneFix) are well known for utilizing this effect prior to amplification.<|txt_split|>It is difficult to get a stable pulse using the gain switch approach. This generates many constraints on the laser diode choice, the laser diode integration (fiber coupling) and the driver electronic performance. The energy reached by these laser diodes is typically on the order of 10 picojoules, so reaching 1 mJ requires 80 dB gain. Knowing that a typical amplifier stage gain is approximately 15 dB, we take roughly 5 stages of amplification with all of the isolators, ASE filters, and laser diode pumps that every stage must contain in order to use this approach to create a picosecond fiber laser.

children.title: External modulation seeder:
children.metadata: Fiber Modulator Basics; EOMs; Laser Diode Pulsing; Peak Power Limitation; Low Energy Seeder Pulses; Amplifying Cost
children.content: Another way to achieve short pulses is to use very fast external fiber modulators like EOMs (ElectroOptics Modulators) as described in our tutorial "Fiber Modulator Basics". It is possible to overcome the peak power limitation of such components by pulsing the source laser diode. In any case, the pulse generation approach makes the energy of each seeder pulse very low, which makes the amplifying part very costly.

children.title: - Microchip seeder:
children.metadata: Q-switched crystal cavity; Microchip seeders; SESAM saturable absorbers; 808 nm wavelength; nanosecond to picosecond pulses; repetition rate control
children.content: A third way of making short pulse seeders for fiber lasers uses the Q-switch effect in a crystal cavity. These elements utilize a crystal gain media (such as Nd:YAG or Nd:YO4) with a saturable absorber.<|txt_split|>These components, called "Microchip seeders", have historically been used for nanosecond pulse generation with \(\mathrm{C + 4 + YAG}\) as a saturable absorber), generating typically 3-30ns pulses at a given repetition rate.<|txt_split|>More recently, very fast saturable absorbers have been developed using semiconductor technology. These are generally called SESAM (Semiconductor Saturable Absorber Media). Components like the ones provided by Batop are now extensively used and yield pulses of less than 30 picoseconds. 808 nm is a useful wavelength for this purpose. Pushing the pump in the 100s of nanosecond range is a good way to control the repetition rate.

children.title: Figure 24: Typical microchip seeder used for picosecond pulse generation. Nanosecond pulsed \(808~\mathrm{nm}\) single mode pump can be used here to be able to control the microchip repetition rate.
children.metadata: Microchip seeder; nanosecond pulses; 808 nm pump; 1064 nm output; SESAM; Nd:YVO4; gain; repetition rate control
children.content: The diagram illustrates a typical microchip seeder used for picosecond pulse generation. It features a copper heat sink on the left, followed by an Nd:YVO4 gain medium, and a SESAM (Semiconductor Saturable Absorber Mirror) positioned downstream. An 808 nm pump laser (indicated by a green arrow) enters the system to excite the gain medium, while the 1064 nm output (indicated by a red arrow) exits from the right. The setup enables precise control of the microchip’s repetition rate through the SESAM, which acts as a mode-locking element.

children.title: f) Pulsed fiber lasers (femtosecond range: "Ultrafast")
children.metadata: Ultrafast lasers; Athermal laser-matter interaction; Semiconductor machining; Eye surgery applications; Smartphone industry; MOPA laser architectures
children.content: The final category of fiber lasers discussed in this tutorial takes us into the very complicated world of laser lasers. Ultrafast lasers are typically in the pulse width range of 100 femtoseconds to a few picoseconds.<|txt_split|>These sources are one of major interest for a lot of laser-matter interaction applications because the laser-matter effect is "athermal". The matter is directly transformed from the solid state to the plasma state without passing through the liquid state. This provides very high-resolution machining and is now extensively used in the semiconductor, eye surgery and smartphone industries.<|txt_split|>Typical amplified MOPA architectures are also used here. However, there are two important principles that are important to consider:

children.title: The Mode-locking principle is the basis of the seeder "oscillator
children.metadata: Heisenberg principle; ultrashort pulses; broad spectral bandwidth; cavity modes; Fourier transform; modelocked cavities; pulse repetition rate; AOM; EOM; SESAM; industrial fiber laser
children.content: The Heisenberg principle states that the product of the amplification spectral bandwidth and the pulse duration cannot go below a given value. In other words, ultrashort pulses mean wide spectral emission bandwidth. 100's of femtosecond pulses means \(10^{\prime}\mathrm{s}\) of nm of spectral bandwidth. Ultrashort lasers always have a broad emission bandwidth with many cavity modes.<|txt_split|>The Fourier transform of a given comb of emission modes gives an ultrashort pulse only when the various modes are all in phase. Therefore, making an ultrashort pulse laser consists of building a broad amplification cavity and adding some elements which make the cavity modes emit in phase by combining the beam with the cavity. Active modulators such as AOM (Acuto-opto Modulator) or an EOM (Electro-Optic Modulator) can be used. Passive versions like the SESAM (Semiconductor Saturable Absorber Media) are generally the best solution for building an industrial fiber based ultrafast oscillator.<|txt_split|>Modelocked cavities have a direct relation between the cavity length and the ultrashift pulse repetition rate. Typical modelocked oscillators will have a pulse repetition rate in the 1-100 MHz range.

subnode.title: The Mode-locking principle is the basis of the seeder "oscillator_1
subnode.metadata: Heisenberg principle; ultrashort pulses; broad spectral bandwidth; cavity modes; Fourier transform; phase alignment; AOM; EOM; SESAM; industrial fiber oscillator
subnode.content: The Heisenberg principle states that the product of the amplification spectral bandwidth and the pulse duration cannot go below a given value. In other words, ultrashort pulses mean wide spectral emission bandwidth. 100's of femtosecond pulses means \(10^{\prime}\mathrm{s}\) of nm of spectral bandwidth. Ultrashort lasers always have a broad emission bandwidth with many cavity modes.The Fourier transform of a given comb of emission modes gives an ultrashort pulse only when the various modes are all in phase. Therefore, making an ultrashort pulse laser consists of building a broad amplification cavity and adding some elements which make the cavity modes emit in phase by combining the beam with the cavity. Active modulators such as AOM (Acuto-opto Modulator) or an EOM (Electro-Optic Modulator) can be used. Passive versions like the SESAM (Semiconductor Saturable Absorber Media) are generally the best solution for building an industrial fiber based ultrafast oscillator.

subnode.title: The Mode-locking principle is the basis of the seeder "oscillator_2
subnode.metadata: Modelocked cavities; cavity length; ultrashift pulse repetition rate; pulse repetition rate; oscillator frequency
subnode.content: Modelocked cavities have a direct relation between the cavity length and the ultrashift pulse repetition rate. Typical modelocked oscillators will have a pulse repetition rate in the 1-100 MHz range.

children.title: Optical Parametric Chirped-Pulse Amplification (OPCPA)
children.metadata: Pulse picking; High-frequency amplification; Pulse stretching via dispersion; Hollow core fiber; Nonlinear effects mitigation; Fiber amplifier design; Ultrafast laser systems; Synchronization tools; Pulse repetition rate control; Nonlinear simulation software
children.content: Amplifying a modelocked oscillator signal requires the amplification of a high frequency signal of very short pulses. It generates three major difficulties:<|txt_split|>- First, if one wants to get enough energy to have an impact on the matter (i.e. more than 1 microuole), keeping a \(50\mathrm{MHz}\) repetition rate would require \(50~\mathrm{W}\), which will probably reduce the pulse repetition power for a very small effect on matter. It is thus preferable to reduce the pulse repetition rate of the oscillator by picking some pulses and reducing the repetition rate to the kHz range.<|txt_split|>One thing to remember is that ultrafast oscillators are generally in the range of MHz/nJ. This is because the amplifier amplifies lasers which are useful for laser micromachining are generally in the kHz/μJ range.<|txt_split|>The pulse-picking is generally operated by a fibered or non-fibered AOM (Acousto Optic Modulator).<|txt_split|>AeroDIODE developed a universal tool for pulse picking synchronization. It allows the user to select a desired low frequency synchronized with an input clock signal coming from a photodiode.<|txt_split|>- Second, amplifying very short pulses can have two consequences. When considering a crystal amplifier, the peak power quickly reaches the damage threshold. When considering a fiber amplifier media, some nonlinear effects can quickly destroy the pulse properties. It is therefore necessary to stretch the pulse width in such a way that it is possible to go back to a short pulse after the amplification process. The effect which is utilized in this case is called "dispersion". It stretches the pulse by coupling its spectral and temporal properties (one "color" at the beginning of the pulse and the other "color" at the end of the pulse). A special "hollow core" fiber can be used to stretch the pulse before amplification whereas spatial gratings are generally used to compress the amplified pulse and reach the ultrafast amplified pulse properties.<|txt_split|>Third, ultrashort pulse widths within a fiber generate non-linear effects very rapidly and all the Ultrafast amplified fiber lasers combine some fiber parts and some parts which are not fibered. A good non-linear effect simulation software such as Fiberdesk is clearly mandatory here.

children.title: Figure 26: OPCPA Principle (courtesy of S. Witte et al.)
children.metadata: Optical parametric chirped pulse amplification; pulse stretcher; optical parametric amplifier; pulse compressor; S. Witte
children.content: The diagram illustrates the Optical Parametric Chirped Pulse Amplification (OPCPA) principle, showing a sequence of optical components: a pulse stretcher, an optical parametric amplifier, and a pulse compressor. An initial red pulse enters the pulse stretcher, which broadens its spectrum (shown as a yellow-green distribution). The stretched pulse then passes through the optical parametric amplifier, where its intensity is amplified, producing a broader, colored spectrum peak. Finally, the pulse compressor reshapes the pulse into a high-intensity, narrow peak. Arrows indicate the direction of pulse propagation through the system. The diagram is labeled as “Figure 26: OPCPA Principle (courtesy of S. Witte et al.)”.

subnode.title: Optical Parametric Chirped-Pulse Amplification (OPCPA)_1
subnode.metadata: High-frequency pulse amplification; Modelocked oscillator; Energy limitation; Pulse repetition rate reduction; kHz operation; Pulse selection
subnode.content: Amplifying a modelocked oscillator signal requires the amplification of a high frequency signal of very short pulses. It generates three major difficulties:- First, if one wants to get enough energy to have an impact on the matter (i.e. more than 1 microuole), keeping a \(50\mathrm{MHz}\) repetition rate would require \(50~\mathrm{W}\), which will probably reduce the pulse repetition power for a very small effect on matter. It is thus preferable to reduce the pulse repetition rate of the oscillator by picking some pulses and reducing the repetition rate to the kHz range.

subnode.title: Optical Parametric Chirped-Pulse Amplification (OPCPA)_3
subnode.metadata: pulse amplification; damage threshold; nonlinear effects; pulse stretching; dispersion; hollow core fiber; spatial gratings; ultrafast pulses
subnode.content: - Second, amplifying very short pulses can have two consequences. When considering a crystal amplifier, the peak power quickly reaches the damage threshold. When considering a fiber amplifier media, some nonlinear effects can quickly destroy the pulse properties. It is therefore necessary to stretch the pulse width in such a way that it is possible to go back to a short pulse after the amplification process. The effect which is utilized in this case is called "dispersion". It stretches the pulse by coupling its spectral and temporal properties (one "color" at the beginning of the pulse and the other "color" at the end of the pulse). A special "hollow core" fiber can be used to stretch the pulse before amplification whereas spatial gratings are generally used to compress the amplified pulse and reach the ultrafast amplified pulse properties.

children.title: IV. A modular fiber laser electronics to make nearly all fiber lasers architectures:
children.metadata: Electronic drivers; Fiber laser architectures; Parameter control; Compact integration; Rapid development
children.content: AeroDiode has developed an entire range of electronic drivers able to build nearly any of the fiber laser architectures described above.
